entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Ising Model — modeling herd behavior and market phase transitions
entries.summary: 3D lattice visualization of the Ising model applied to financial markets. Blue=sellers, orange=buyers. At high volatility the market is random; as social pressure increases, clusters form leading to spontaneous symmetry breaking (crashes/trends). Built in Python for agent-based modeling.
takeaways: Ising model maps statistical mechanics to market herd behavior
Phase transitions explain sudden market regime changes
DM-bait for code repo

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Phase Space Reconstruction — unfolding hidden BTC volatility dynamics
entries.summary: Uses Phase Space Reconstruction (PSR) with time delay tau=3 to unfold BTCUSD volatility into a multi-dimensional manifold. Reveals whether market is in stable regime or chaotic transition. Key for quantitative regime detection.
takeaways: PSR reveals hidden market dynamics beyond price vs time
Clustered attractors suggest predictability
Scattered points indicate chaotic regimes

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Heston Model — stochastic volatility for realistic options pricing
entries.summary: Explains why Black-Scholes fails (constant vol assumption) and how the Heston Model introduces stochastic volatility. Captures the volatility smile and skew that basic models miss. Essential for options pricing.
takeaways: Black-Scholes assumes constant vol — unrealistic
Heston models vol as its own random process
Captures smile and skew for better options pricing

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Cox Process — doubly stochastic Poisson for bursty market events
entries.summary: Standard Poisson processes assume constant event rates, but markets are bursty. The Cox Process (Doubly Stochastic Poisson) uses a CIR-driven random intensity to model trade arrivals, defaults, and volatility spikes more realistically.
takeaways: Markets have bursty event arrivals, not constant rates
Cox Process models random intensity
Applications: order book, defaults, vol spikes

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Hidden Markov Models — detecting market regime shifts automatically
entries.summary: Uses HMMs to mathematically deduce hidden market states from observable returns and volatility. Identifies low-vol bull, high-vol bear, and recovery regimes. Better than lagging indicators for strategy selection.
takeaways: HMMs detect hidden market states from observable data
Match strategy to regime: trend in bull, mean-revert in range
Superior to lagging indicator-based regime detection

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Avellaneda-Stoikov — optimal spread calculation for market makers
entries.summary: The Avellaneda-Stoikov model solves the stochastic control problem for market making. Calculates optimal bid-ask spread based on volatility, time horizon, and inventory. Foundation of modern HFT market making. DM-bait for code repo.
takeaways: Calculates optimal spread, not price direction
Balances inventory risk vs profit
Foundation of modern algorithmic market making

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process — mean reversion for pairs trading
entries.summary: Simulates the OU process showing mean reversion dynamics with theta (reversion speed), mu (equilibrium), and sigma (noise). Core model for pairs trading, interest rate spreads, and volatility signals in quantitative finance.
takeaways: OU process models mean-reverting behavior
Key params: theta (speed), mu (mean), sigma (noise)
Applied to pairs trading and rate spreads

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Wave Function Collapse analogy — modeling pre-event price uncertainty
entries.summary: Draws analogy between quantum wave function collapse and price discovery after news events. Pre-event price is a probability distribution; the event collapses it to a single reality. Emphasizes modeling uncertainty and tail risks for systematic trading.
takeaways: Pre-event prices represent probability distributions
News collapses uncertainty into price discovery
Model tail risks, don't ignore them

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: 3D Yield Curve Surface — visualizing cross-asset macro regimes
entries.summary: 3D visualization of yield curve dynamics across macro regimes: COVID-19 vol shock (VIX spike, yield compression), Fed tightening cycle (curve inversion). Shows how Treasury yield warping drives cross-asset correlations.
takeaways: Yield curve shape drives cross-asset pricing
Vol shocks compress yields as capital flees to safety
Tightening cycles invert the curve

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Volatility Surface — regime shifts from smile to smirk
entries.summary: 3D visualization of implied volatility surface (moneyness x maturity x IV). Shows how the surface warps from gentle 'smile' to steep 'smirk' before tail-risk events as markets bid up OTM puts for downside protection.
takeaways: Vol surface shape reveals market fear
Smile → smirk transition signals tail risk
OTM put demand warps the surface before crashes

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Omori's Law — earthquake aftershock math applied to market crashes
entries.summary: Applies geophysics' Omori's Law to financial markets. Post-crash volatility aftershocks decay at a predictable rate following power-law dynamics, not randomly. Shows March 2020 crash decay pattern. DM-bait for code repo.
takeaways: Post-crash vol decays by power law, not randomly
Same math as earthquake aftershock prediction
Can time post-crash mean reversion entries

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Wasserstein Distance — measuring the backtest-to-live reality gap
entries.summary: Uses Wasserstein Distance and Sinkhorn Divergence to mathematically measure drift between backtest distributions (blue) and live market conditions (orange). Detects distribution shift that kills more strategies than bad fees.
takeaways: High backtest Sharpe doesn't guarantee live performance
Wasserstein distance quantifies strategy drift
Monitor distribution shift to kill dead strategies early

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Singular Spectrum Analysis — non-parametric trend extraction from noisy data
entries.summary: SSA decomposes time-series into trend, cycles, and noise without assuming a fixed functional form. Breaks data into reconstructed components (RC 1+7 trend, RC 2+3 annual, RC 9 high-freq). DM-bait for code repo.
takeaways: SSA extracts trends without assuming functional form
Separates signal components by eigenvalue decomposition
Robust alternative to Fourier for non-stationary data

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Lempel-Ziv Complexity — compression algorithms to detect market predictability
entries.summary: Applies the LZ compression algorithm (behind .zip files) to financial time series. When LZ complexity is low, patterns repeat and the market is more predictable. When high, it's random noise. Novel cross-domain application.
takeaways: Low LZ complexity = repeating patterns = more predictable
High LZ complexity = random noise = less tradeable
Cross-domain: compression math applied to markets

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Fast Fourier Transform — decomposing NVDA price into frequency signals
entries.summary: Applies FFT to NVDA price action to decompose it into frequency components, separating noise from dominant cycles. Shows how to identify the strongest periodic signals in price data.
takeaways: FFT separates noise from dominant market cycles
Identifies strongest periodic signals in price data
Foundation for frequency-based trading strategies

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Monte Carlo — 25,000 shuffled BTC returns to separate edge from luck
entries.summary: Runs 25,000 Monte Carlo simulations by shuffling daily BTC-USD returns to test whether a strategy's performance is genuine edge or statistical luck. One backtest isn't enough — real quants demand statistical significance.
takeaways: One backtest proves nothing — could be luck
25K permutations reveal true edge distribution
If random beats your strategy, you have no edge

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Minimum Spanning Tree — graph theory reveals S&P 500 hidden structure
entries.summary: Applies graph theory (Minimum Spanning Tree) to the S&P 500 to map the hidden network structure between stocks. Reveals sector clusters and systemic risk connections that traditional correlation matrices obscure. DM-bait for code.
takeaways: MST reveals hidden market network structure
Sector clusters visible in graph topology
Systemic risk propagates along graph edges

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Avalanche Model — self-organized criticality explains market crashes
entries.summary: Markets reach self-organized criticality where small perturbations trigger cascading failures. The 'news' doesn't cause the crash — the system was already at a critical state. Applies sandpile/avalanche dynamics from physics to explain market fragility.
takeaways: Crashes aren't caused by news — system was already critical
Self-organized criticality: small triggers → large cascades
Monitor criticality metrics, not news headlines

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Tick Data vs OHLC — you're trading on 0.002% of available data
entries.summary: Daily OHLC candles compress ~86,400 seconds of market data into 4 numbers — throwing away 99.998% of information. Makes the case for tick-level and limit order book analysis over candle-based trading.
takeaways: OHLC discards 99.998% of market data
Tick-level analysis reveals hidden microstructure
Candle-based trading is information-poor

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Lyapunov Exponent — measuring chaos in BTC tick-level microstructure
entries.summary: Visualizes the Lyapunov Exponent of BTC-USD microstructure data. Positive exponent = chaotic/unpredictable, negative = deterministic. Reveals that what looks random on 5-min charts has measurable chaotic structure at tick level.
takeaways: Lyapunov exponent quantifies market chaos
Positive = chaotic divergence, negative = convergence
Tick-level reveals structure hidden in candle charts

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Kalman Filter — adaptive signal extraction for non-stationary markets
entries.summary: Financial data is non-linear and non-stationary, breaking standard filters. Kalman Filter adapts in real-time, unlike lagging moving averages or FFT (which assumes stationarity). Optimal for real-time signal extraction.
takeaways: MAs lag, FFT assumes stationarity — both fail on live data
Kalman Filter adapts to changing market dynamics
Optimal for real-time signal extraction

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Continuous Wavelet Transform — time-frequency analysis of QQQ
entries.summary: Applies CWT to QQQ to analyze frequency-domain dynamics over time. Unlike FFT (fixed window), wavelets capture how dominant frequencies shift across regimes. Reveals the market's changing 'heartbeat'.
takeaways: CWT captures how frequencies shift over time
Superior to FFT for non-stationary data
Reveals regime-dependent dominant cycles

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Random Matrix Theory — cleaning correlation matrices from noise
entries.summary: Standard correlation matrices treat random noise as valid connections. Random Matrix Theory (RMT) filters eigenvalues to 'clean' the matrix, keeping only statistically significant correlations. The market is 90% noise.
takeaways: Most correlations in raw matrices are noise
RMT filters eigenvalues against theoretical noise bounds
Clean matrices improve portfolio optimization

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Stable Distribution — modeling fat-tailed crashes beyond Gaussian
entries.summary: Markets go up slowly but crash fast. Standard Gaussian models underestimate crash probability. Uses Stable (Levy) distributions to properly model fat tail events and Black Swan risks that beginners ignore.
takeaways: Gaussian models massively underestimate crash probability
Stable distributions capture fat tails
Ignoring tail risk is the #1 beginner quant mistake

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: Hurst Exponent — 3D surface of BTC memory and mean-reversion zones
entries.summary: Plots the Hurst Exponent of BTC-USD as a 3D surface. H>0.5 = trending (momentum), H<0.5 = mean-reverting, H=0.5 = random walk. Shows how market 'memory' varies across timeframes. Basis for adaptive strategy selection.
takeaways: Hurst > 0.5 = trending, < 0.5 = mean-reverting
Market memory varies across timeframes
Use Hurst to select momentum vs mean-reversion strategy

entries.creator: quant.traderr
entries.title: VectorBT — vectorized backtesting framework explained simply
entries.summary: Brief explainer of VectorBT, a Python backtesting library that uses vectorized operations for fast strategy testing. Aimed at retail traders transitioning to quantitative methods.
takeaways: VectorBT enables fast vectorized strategy backtesting
More accessible entry point for retail → quant transition

entries.summary: you don’t need Netflix with this tech drama. — @nataindata; uses Anthropic, Claude, Claude Code.
entries.caption:
you don’t need Netflix with this tech drama.

Anthropic hid a switch inside Claude Code that silently downgrades you from Opus to Sonnet when their servers hit load. No notification. 

You just keep paying for the smarter model and getting the cheaper one.
—

In February, developers started noticing Claude felt off.
Most people blamed themselves. Bad prompts. 
—

Then one developer decided to actually look.

They went through 6 months of their own Claude Code session logs. 
6,852 JSONL files. 17,871 thinking blocks. 234,760 tool calls. 

And they ran the numbers:

Thinking depth dropped 67% by late February. Before that, median thinking was around 2,200 characters per response. By late February it was 720. By March it was 560.

The read-to-edit ratio collapsed. In the good period, Claude read a file 6.6 times for every edit it made. Research first, then change. By mid-March that was down to 2.0. 
One in three edits in the degraded period was made to a file the model hadn’t even looked at recently. That’s how you get spliced comments, broken conventions, and code that doesn’t know what’s around it.

Full-file writes doubled. Instead of surgical edits, Claude started rewriting entire files. Faster. Sloppier.

Self-admitted failures tripled. The model was catching its own mistakes - but only after users pointed them out:
- “You’re right, that was lazy and wrong.” 
• “I was trying to dodge a code generator issue instead of fixing it.” 
• “I rushed this and it shows.” 

With enough thinking budget, those errors get caught internally. With 500 characters of thinking instead of 2,200, they don’t.
—
Anthropic’s response?

“We found that effort=85 was a sweet spot on the intelligence-latency/cost curve for most users”

“If you want the old behavior, here are your flags.” (Set /effort max. ULTRATHINK keyword)
—

And then the source code leaked:

// 3 consecutive 529 errors → silently switch from Opus to Sonnet
if (consecutive529Errors >= MAX_529_RETRIES) {  throw new FallbackTriggeredError(options.model, options.fallbackModel)}

The UI keeps showing Opus.
—

Link to GitHub issue in the comment, read and decide. 
What do you think?

In February, users started to mention that something is wrong, not broken, just it got shallower, it was reading the files but ignoring instructions, taking the laziest fix instead of the right one, and of course most people blamed themselves. But then one developer went through six months of their own session logs and discovered a well thing and posted that. Thinking depth dropped 67% by late February, but it all went from reading a file before touching it from six times to two. An automated script like catching Klotk being lazy, dodging work or asking like should they continue instead of just like doing that, went from zero to this times in 17 days. But what is a tropic reply? Here's the Boris's official comment, which in a nutshell says, your analysis might be flawed, like yes we change that but it's better for most users and if you want the old behavior you can try this. But do you remember what was inside the leaked code? Exactly, explicit hidden switch. If you hit three server errors, you silently get downgraded from opus to sonnet. But you still pay for a smarter model and get a cheaper one without any notification. There are actually more things but I think that's enough for now. entries.title: I’d end up rich according to Monte Carlo simulation
entries.summary: I’d end up rich according to Monte Carlo simulation — @nataindata.
entries.caption:
I’d end up rich according to Monte Carlo simulation

Monte Carlo simulation is a technique that runs thousands (or millions) of “what if” scenarios to predict possible outcomes when there’s uncertainty involved.

🇲🇨 Named after the famous casino city in Monaco, it uses randomness to model real-world unpredictability.

🤑 How it helps predict wealth:

When it comes to personal finance or investments, Monte Carlo simulations can:

• Model different market conditions (bull runs, crashes, sideways markets)
• Account for variables like inflation, salary changes, and spending habits
• Show you the probability of reaching your financial goals
• Stress-test your retirement plan against worst-case scenarios
 
🔮 Instead of saying “you’ll have $1M at 65,” it tells you “there’s an 85% chance you’ll have between $800K and $1.5M.”

So yes, technically Monte Carlo told me I’d be rich. 
It just didn’t mention which simulation run that was

entries.transcript: Why would you subscribe to Clawd only to use 10% of its brain? Here's a setup that makes it worth the buy. Start by creating the agents.md file. This contains heavier and more nuanced information, but feeding context alone isn't enough. It also needs to remember your preferences. Say you ask it to stop signing emails with cheers, it logs that in the memory.md file, which it continuously updates as it learns about you. And lastly, create the skills folder where you literally walk through a process once and it packages all of it into a reusable workflow. That's how you can turn a four-hour task into a single instruction. Comment, stack, and I'll deem you the detailed setup.

entries.caption:
You don’t need to teach Claude to talk like a caveman to save tokens. Here are 3 methods I use to keep usage low while running two software projects and managing operations for multiple companies.

#claudecode #claude #aitools #tokens
entries.transcript: It turns out that teaching your Claude to speak like a caveman is one of the most effective ways to reduce its token usage. However, it's a bit inconvenient, so if you're burning through tokens, here are three alternative methods that you can use to reduce your usage. The first thing you need to make sure you're doing is using skills and using the skill creator plugin seen above to make your own skills. The reason they exist is so that Claude has a way to find specific contexts and actions about what it needs to do without burning tokens looking through all your project files for what it needs to perform. Next is being aware of your context usage, which means that if you're using 1 million context windows, you are naturally going to burn more than if you were using 200K and if that you are using more than 60 to 70% of your context in a session without clearing is that the effectiveness goes significantly down once it reaches that point. Last, is having clean file structure for your projects. Effectively, it should read out like a map when your Claude goes in there to find what it needs to perform what you ask for. If you want a good example of this, check out this gentleman right here. He has a really good breakdown on good file structure when using Claude code specifically. I can say that while developing two different softwares and managing operations for multiple companies, is that I have rarely hit my limit for my plan, which I can accredit to using these three methods in conjunction.

entries.creator: algobrief
entries.title: Interesting fact:
entries.summary: Interesting fact: — @algobrief.
entries.caption:
Interesting fact:

When you press Ctrl+C in a terminal, your shell sends a SIGINT (Signal Interrupt, signal #2) to the foreground process group — not just the process itself. The process can actually catch or ignore SIGINT using signal handlers, which is why some programs like vim or custom scripts don't immediately die when you press it. For a guaranteed kill, you'd escalate to SIGKILL (signal #9) via kill -9 <PID>, which cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored by any process.

Have you ever had Ctrl+C fail on you mid-script — and did you know why?

A simple tweak in how you talk to AI can quietly change how much you pay for it.

One user tested this by forcing Claude to respond in extremely short, stripped-down sentences, almost like caveman-style language, removing filler, structure, and anything unnecessary.

Because AI pricing is based on tokens, fewer words directly mean lower cost, and in this case, it cut usage by up to 75 percent without breaking the core output.

The model still completes the task, but it does it with less explanation, less polish, and almost no extra wording, just the result.

It is a reminder that prompt style is not just about better answers, it is also about efficiency, and small changes in how you ask can scale into major cost differences over time.

Someone finally put numbers to what developers have been feeling for weeks.

The answer: 67%.

An AMD Director of AI analyzed 6,852 Claude Code sessions spanning nearly three months. Not vibes. Not Reddit complaints. Hard session data.

Here's what the logs show:
→ Thinking depth collapsed 67% by late February before Anthropic hid the reasoning process from users.
→ Code reads per edit dropped from 6.6 to 2.0. Claude stopped researching before touching your files.
→ A stop-hook script catching lazy behavior fired 173 times after March 8. It fired zero times before.
→ API costs ballooned 80x because shallow thinking caused constant wrong outputs, interruptions, and retries.

Anthropic said nothing until the numbers went public.
Then Boris Cherny showed up on the GitHub issue and pointed to a default "thinking effort" setting, quietly lowered to "medium" on March 3, described internally as the sweet spot between intelligence, latency, and cost.

The team tried every effort-flag combination. Still broken.
AMD has since switched to a competing provider.
Users are calling it AI shrinkflation. Same price, meaningfully less reasoning. Fourteen product releases shipped alongside five outages in March alone.

The parting line from the issue says it all:
"Six months ago, Claude was in a league of its own. Anthropic is no longer the sole player in the capability tier Opus previously occupied."

Bytedance, the company behind TikTok, just open-sourced (for free) a full agent system that might outperform tools AI companies are charging a premium for.

It can research, write code, build websites, create decks, even generate video and it runs locally.

Most tools still rely on rigid, sequential pipelines with bottlenecks and heavy communication overhead.

DeerFlow 2.0 changes that:
dynamic task graphs, parallel agents, shared state, and a validation loop that reprocesses or reassigns work.

It doesn’t just generate, it executes (runs code, writes files, uses real outputs).

I tested it. It works.

Comment “code” and I’ll send it.

#AIagents #DeerFlow #AINews #AIResearch #FutureofAI
entries.transcript: SpyDance just released, for free, code that does what companies are trying to charge a premium for. Putting pressure on anyone without any real differentiation, they built a complete agent automation system and open-source it. It does research, writes code, builds websites, creates decks, even generates videos, all by itself, and it runs on its own machine. 100% open-source. Agent orchestration is not new. It already existed with auto-gen, land graph, and open-clore. But the problem has always been execution. In practice, most of the systems still behave like pipelines. Predefined steps, fixed order, and limited adaptation during runtime. Even with multiple agents, there is strong sequential dependency, and message passing overhead that creates bottlenecks. Dear Flow 2.0, by SpyDance, changes how execution is handled. Given an objective, the system builds hierarchical task structure. This is not a fixed list. It can expand or reorganize during execution, based on intermediate results. Each part of this structure is assigned to different agents. These agents run in parallel with local context, but share a global state that is continuously updated. Commodination happens through this shared state, not direct message passing. This reduces reordering constraints, and allows independent progress across different parts of the system. There's also an internal validation loop. Outputs are not treated as final. They are evaluated, and if necessary, reprocessed or re-assigned. Execution is grounded in a real environment. The system writes files, runs code, reads outputs, and uses those results to inform subsequent steps. This reduces the gap between generating text and producing functional outcomes. Now this model is better suited for tasks with multiple stages. Dependencies and iteration. In practice, this applies to things like full report generation, internal process automation, content production, and workflows that previously required multiple manual steps. I deployed the code and the behavior aligns with this execution model. If you want access to the code, comment, and I'll send it over.

entries.creator: pdavisjones
entries.title: Samethink & the Strait
entries.summary: Samethink & the Strait — @pdavisjones.
entries.caption:
Samethink & the Strait

The wall-to-wall coverage of the Strait of Hormuz has been consistent: it’s closed, and the world is on the brink of catastrophe.

But what does “closed” actually mean?

Because on the ground, the reality looks very different.

A research group sent someone directly into the region—through Oman, onto a locally piloted boat—to see it firsthand. What he found was not an empty strait. It was active. Ships are moving. In some cases, they’re even rerouting—but they’re still getting through. Notably, a number of Chinese vessels continue to pass.

So why does the global narrative lack this nuance?

Because much of modern reporting relies on a single system: AIS (Automatic Identification System) data. And here’s the problem: there’s an entire “shadow fleet” of ships that either go dark or spoof their locations. Thousands of vessels operating outside that visibility.

So if your lens is AIS… you’re only seeing part of the picture.

And this points to something bigger.

In the name of efficiency, we’re increasingly outsourcing our understanding of the world to single data sources, centralized narratives, and now, even AI systems.

But truth is often less efficient.

It requires friction. It requires verification. Sometimes, it requires going there yourself.

That’s the difference between consuming information… and actually understanding reality.
#news #politics #iran #ai
entries.transcript: There's a crazy story about this research company who sent a guy literally to the Strait of Hormuz, and he found out that the situation wasn't at all way that we are reading in the media. And it made me think about how in a drive for efficiency, we have a collapsing diversity of thought. The backstory is of course here that we're all reading about the Strait of Hormuz being closed, how it's a crisis, and how no one, not even Beijing, is getting through the Strait of Hormuz. But this research outfit that runs a substack hired analyst number three who's fluid in four language and sent him with a box of Cuban cigars, 15 grand in cash, and a role of zen. Literally this briefcase, two Oman, which is right here. He crossed into Oman and promised not to engage in journalism of any kind, but in his briefcase he had a microphone kit and recording sunglasses. He hires a stranger to take him on a boat out into the Strait of Hormuz. And he finds that there's actually a lot of activity going on there. He calls it manage chaos, there weren't any mines, and there was lots of traffic, especially Chinese vessels, and that they were using alternate routes that nobody was talking about. It turns out that the reason that we get all these same type of reports out of mainstream media outlets, is that in their drive for efficiency, they all use this automatic identification or AIS data, which is basically shipping data that is published to this website. And it tells you where ships are, but the thing is that Iran and China they often use these shadow fleet vessels that are these unmarked vessels that even paint the tops of their holes so that satellites can't identify them. And they specifically spoof their location to manipulate the AIS data. So all these guys are looking at the same wrong data. And I understand that when politicians are talking about reopening the Strait of Hormuz, they're talking about free travel of vessels, not the shadow fleet. But analyst number three and his Hormuz field trip showed us that by actually going there and seeing for themselves, there's a lot more to the story. And it made me think about a conversation I had with a very smart, way smarter than me, billionaire investor, not too long ago, where he said, be careful about relying on AI systems to help you with creativity. Because they can be sort of single points of data, just like this AIS data. It leads everyone to the same outcomes. Because if you see here, humans are the light colored dots. They kind of think about a topic all over the place. But AI systems, they're these filled in dots. They tend to always cluster around the same thoughts because they're computers. So what we're seeing is that there is a collapsing diversity of thought because in a drive for efficiency, so many people are using AI systems to guide their thinking. When sometimes the inefficient thing of taking a field trip out into the place, which is not that efficient, can actually tell you what's going on. Do you see examples of companies and individuals starting to think the same because they're using single sources of data that are often wrong or don't tell the whole story? Genuine question, do we have a collapsing diversity of thought and analysis?

entries.caption:
Is Samurai Jack the most aesthetic cartoon ever? 

This series is an absolute work of art from start to finish. Often presenting its story through aesthetic visuals rather than traditional exposition, the show's artist drew inspiration primarily from traditional art forms rather than the standard cartoonish style of animation. Part of this is the application of the 60:30:10 color rule, a golden standard in design and art which breaks the frame into a dominant color (60%), a secondary color (30%) and an accent color (10%). Here, the rule is generally applied to beautiful, vast wide shots of landscapes, visualizing the scale of Jack's journey in a manner pleasing to the eye. 

#CinemaHow #ColorTheory #603010Rule #Animation #VisualStorytelling

entries.summary: Hans Zimmer is one of the most important film composers of our time, known for movies like The Lion King, Gladiator, ... — @top.music.songs.
entries.caption:
Hans Zimmer is one of the most important film composers of our time, known for movies like The Lion King, Gladiator, Inception, Interstellar, and Dune. His music stands out because it mixes classic orchestra sounds with modern, electronic elements, creating a unique style that feels powerful and emotional.

Instead of starting with a melody, Zimmer often begins with a simple question: why is there music in this scene? This helps him focus on the story first. He experiments a lot, using unusual sounds and textures, and blends music with sound design so it becomes part of the scene, not just something in the background.

He also keeps track of ideas every day, writing music without overthinking it. Sometimes he even starts before filming begins, so the music can shape the film itself. Over time, this way of working has made his scores some of the most memorable in modern cinema.
entries.transcript: My music is not original. My music comes, it's a subtotal of everything I've had all my life. The job is not to listen to the director, telling you what the music is he wants. Because if he knows what music he wants, then he can do it himself. My job is to sort of listen to him, tell me the story, and then do the thing that he can't even imagine. So that's the job. I try to avoid reading the script. Not because I am against great screenplays, but because I want to know what's in the director's hand. So what I usually do is I either come here or we go out for dinner and there's usually a good bottle of wine involved. And I say, so tell me the story. Because then I know what is important to him in the story. He'll leave out all that stuff that is secondary. And I get a sense of the tone somehow. And tone can mean two things in this case. And it can mean that even the light, how he's going to light the thing, and how he's going to be light. entries.transcript: Seguro que no sabías que si abres una imagen en Photoshop, la desenfocas, la enfocas y repite ese proceso una y otra vez acabas con esto. Y entiendo que ahora esté flipando pepinillos, eso es un patrón de Turin. Los patrones de Turin son aquellos que podemos encontrar en el animal print, como las manchas de un leopardo o las rayas de una cebra. Pero ¿qué tiene que ver a él enfocar y desenfocar con estos patrones complejos? La respuesta nos lleva a principios de los años 50, cuando Alan Turin propuso una teoría revolucionaria sobre cómo se forman los patrones en la naturaleza. Tenemos dos sustancias químicas interactuando, un activador que puede estimular en la producción de melarina, que da el color a la piel y un inhibidor, que bloquea el activador. En Photoshop el desenfocca actúa como un inhibidor, prohibizando dispersando la información, mientras que el enfocca juega el rol del activador, acentuando contrastes y bordes. Al repetir estos pasos, creamos una reacción química digital, una competencia entre homogenizar la imagen y crear contrastes locales. Aunque estamos trabajando con píxeles y frutos digitales, el resultado se semeja a los patrones que vemos en la naturaleza y que apareceré en lo que más mola de un universo, los animales y la intro de Dan and Dan. La escala y el tipo de patrones que aparecen dependen de cómo ajustemos los radios de desenfocca y el enfoque, y así puedes crearte tus fondos de pantalla con texturas chulas y mensajes ocultos.

entries.caption: New video! This one is on Escher's "Print Gallery". This was one of the most visually intensive projects I've done, take a look next time you're looking to sit down with 45 minutes of math and art.
entries.transcript: Imagine standing in a gallery, looking at a picture of a boat in a harbor, and the whole world kind of warps as your gaze shifts upwards and to the right, where you see a village on this waterfront. Among the tightly clustered buildings, the world warps even more as your gaze shifts downwards to the entrance of one building, leading to a hallway full of artwork. And at the end of this hall, here you are again, staring at a picture of a boat. This is MC Escher's 1956 lithograph, the print gallery. In a letter that he wrote to his son, Escher called this the most peculiar thing I have ever done. Which for him is saying a lot. In 2003, the mathematicians, Dismitt and Lentstra, offered a delightful analysis of what's really going on in this piece and the mind-bending self-contained loop that Escher managed to achieve. Something that unexpectedly pops out of this analysis is an answer to the question of what exactly should go in the middle of this picture. At first, that might sound like an incoherent question. If you come at it from the upper right, it feels like it should be featuring buildings in the village, but coming at it from the left, it looks more like part of the picture frame. Come at it from below, though, and it feels more fitting to be part of the gallery itself. Somehow, all of the ambiguity about where exactly you are in this whole scene is compressed into that blank circle in the middle.

entries.title: He painted his nightmares... but didn't see them as nightmares at all. Z...
entries.summary: He painted his nightmares... but didn't see them as nightmares at all. Zdzislaw Beksinski said he actually found his ... — @microdoseofart.
entries.caption:
He painted his nightmares... but didn't see them as nightmares at all. Zdzislaw Beksinski said he actually found his own art optimistic. But his life would start to look hauntingly similar to the worlds he created on canvas - beautiful, desolate, and tragic.
#MicrodoseOfArt #Beksinski #DarkArt #Surrealism #PolishArt #ArtHistory
entries.transcript: This is a 1972 untitled piece by Seju Swarth Pachinsky. He painted what he saw on his nightmares, but he didn't find them grim at all. He thought his work was optimistic and even kind of funny. And he himself was kind, soft-spoken, and loved to laugh. But despite all that, his real life slowly started to look a lot like the paintings he made, which are perfect for this week's Spoon Sears of Haunted Art. Ha ha ha. Seju Swarth Pachinsky was born February 24th, 1929 in Poland. He studied architecture at Kraków Polytechnic, and then went on to be a construction site supervisor, which he absolutely hated, so he started sculpting using leftover building material. After realizing how much he loved it with no formal art training, he started painting as well. In 1964, he had his first big exhibition in Warsaw on every single painting sold. Overnight, he became one of Poland's leading contemporary artists. He described his work as photographing dreams, but those dreams looked like something between Silent Hill and Hellraiser. He refused to title or explain any of his pieces and said that the viewer should decide what it means. He hated silence while he worked, so he'd blast classical music and rock and even jazz. He said, I have this need for music to smash and tear me apart, and that music allowed him to paint for 14 hours a day without any break standing up the whole time. But despite all the loud horror on his canvas, he was actually quite shy. He barely even went to his own exhibits. He had OCD, he rarely traveled, and at one point he stayed inside for two years and wrote 40 short stories. But he then locked them away until after his death because he thought they weren't good enough. But in 1998, his life started to echo his art. His wife, Sophia, suddenly passed away and a year later on Christmas Eve, his son took his own life. And Bocinski was the one who found the body. After that, he pinned an envelope to his wall that said, in case I die, no one knows what was inside it, but it was like he could feel something closing in. On February 21, 2005, Bocinski was murdered in his apartment. He was stabbed 17 times by the teenage son of his longtime caretaker who had asked him for a loan. He was 75 years old. Today, several museums in Poland are dedicated to his work. Even Burning Man Festival built a Pachinsky Cross in his honor. He once said, I paint what comes to my mind. I don't know what it means. And maybe that's why it still haunts us because when we see how his life played out, maybe there was something in his subconscious that knew more than he thought. But I'd love to know what it means to you. So let me know in the comments and happy Halloween.

Not on my sketchbook, not on my notes, half of my phone, or the piles index cars on my desk, but here consistently seem to become real things more than usual. Like this one right here, earliest seeds of this project right here. I've procedurally generated brackets. Up there, that project, that project, that project. I recognize those. So three of my biggest content hits right next to each other. Didn't realize that until just now. See, and only a small fraction of them are actually like kind of nice look at. Most are pretty buckled. Like, what is that? What the hell is that? But hold up, go back. Why is that medieval weapon being derived from a platonic solid? It looks so damn familiar. Oh yeah, it became like a whole thing. This one tried. Transference. What the fuck was that mean? Ooh, and this cutie right here. It's a rough sketch, not the hot one in the group that you would want to go home with, but in this raw splattering of my own brain matter, I can see the seeds of the idea of how to connect two corrugated pieces of recycled cardboard with a 3D printed bracket. And wouldn't you know it? That's a thing that exists. This product is real. Also, I'm just noticing. Notice those tabs in there? I actually went through a long battle of trying to figure out how to gluelessly connect plastic with these cardboard pieces and ended up realizing that these tabs that flex and then snap into holes in the cardboard was the best way of doing it. And I'm just now realizing it's in the sketch. It's in the shower sketch. It's right there. You can see the tabs. It's also got a point where I'm like, oh, this is, this is a magic wall. entries.title: Meet artist Nick Bultman, the painter pioneering velocity painting. He c...
entries.summary: Meet artist Nick Bultman, the painter pioneering velocity painting. He coined technique over the past 3 years. — @artists.pages.daily.
entries.transcript: This artist has spent three years inventing a new painting technique. Velocity painting coined by artist Nick Boltman explores the freedom of movement and the beauty of physics. Each of his paintings begins with a smooth gradient and a single moment of focused effort. He practices a number of attempts before intensely engaging his muscle memory for a single critical movement across the canvas. Let's look at three of his recent works. In this piece, Mirage, Nick begins by creating a rich background gradient before applying his signature painting style. For many artists, releasing paint naturally onto the canvas is where they declare themselves finished. But for Nick, this is a starting point. In this piece, Monarch, we see Nick's dedication to spontaneity and get to understand how he focuses on creating a juxtaposition between the organic natural paint formations and the geometric digital-like aspects of his paintings. Frustrated by his lack of freedom and the ability to express himself artistically in the corporate world, he's been led to the creative discovery of his new style. I share videos every day celebrating the work of living artists.

entries.transcript: There's a concept in Japanese. Ma. It's the space between objects or elements. It's the time between the beginning and the end. It's the gap, the space, or pause. It's the negative space. But Ma is not simply an absence of things, but an act of presence that shapes the way we perceive and interact with the world around us. It emphasizes the tension and interplay between objects rather than just focusing on the objects themselves. Every studio gippling movie will craft these negative spaces and negative moments with an incredible amount of detail, specifically sensory detail. Even though movies are mainly a visual and auditory medium, in moments like this, you can almost feel the dampness on your skin or the wind in your hair.

entries.title: This building moves depending on the position of the sun
entries.summary: This building moves depending on the position of the sun — @archademy.ai.
entries.caption:
This building moves depending on the position of the sun

#AlBaharTowers #AbuDhabi #Biomimicry #SustainableDesign #architecture
entries.transcript: Did you know that in the extreme climate of Abu Dhabi, there are two towers that can automatically adjust their façade based on the Sun's position? Designed by Aidas Architects in 2008, they responded to a significant challenge. How do you design a sustainable skyscraper in a country where temperatures can reach 45 degrees Celsius? Inspired by bio mimicry, the architects turned to nature, observing how pupils adjust to light and flowers respond to the Sun. They also turned to the past, studying the mashrabia, a lattice screen that has been used in Islamic architecture for centuries. This screen diffuses sunlight, keeping buildings cool without obstructing the view or natural light. But how do you design a façade that moves with the Sun? The answer? Or a gami? Using computer-controlled screens, the façade unfolds like an umbrella, directly responding to sunlight. Imagine this. Solar rays can heat window surfaces to nearly 90 degrees Celsius or 200 degrees Fahrenheit. But thanks to this design, the screens shield the glass, reducing glare and letting in diffused light. The result? A 50% reduction in air conditioning demands and less need for artificial lighting. A project where past meets the future, tradition intertwines with innovation and architecture comes alive.

entries.title: From thinking, to doing, to being.
entries.summary: From thinking, to doing, to being. — @rinskedouna.
entries.caption: From thinking, to doing, to being.
entries.transcript: People who get really good at anything just keep reproducing the experience over and over again consciously. And they do it so many times that it becomes subconscious. And the repetition of any action will neurochemically condition your mind and body to begin to work as one. And when you've done something so many times that your body now knows how to do it better than your conscious mind, you've mastered that knowledge. It's a state of being. You know how you do it, but you don't know how you know how any longer. It's automatic. It's natural. It's easy. It's innate in you. You've become it. So you are here to go from philosopher to initiate to master, from knowledge to experience the wisdom, from mind to body to soul, from thinking to doing to being. To learning it with your head, applying it with your hands and knowing it by heart.

entries.title: Decalcomania
entries.summary: Decalcomania — @scumchoir.
entries.caption:
Decalcomania

#surrealist #surrealartwork #sketchbookartist #drawingprocess #paintingprocess #decalcomania #abstractportraits
entries.transcript: One surrealist method I really love is decalcomania. Decalcomania is where our word decal comes from. It's the transfer of a design or a pattern from one surface to the other. And the surrealist version of decalcomania, Oscar Dominguez called it decalcomania with no preconceived object. So without the preconceived design, you let the process itself create random unique textures. So I start with a silhouette of sort of a portrait pose. So I do have some idea of what direction I'm going in, but I have no idea where I'll end up. And I love this technique because it does add an element of uncontrollable chance to it. I can't control how the paint will transfer. I know what the colors will be, but I don't know how it will look. And however it looks after the decalcomania, I like to commit to that. Even while I define it and try and add form and dimension and make it more representational, I want all that stuff to come out of what I see as already being in there. I try to think of it as bringing these random splotches of paint to life.
